content.full_text:
CITY OF MONTEREY
CITY HALL, MONTEREY, CALIFORNIA 93940

(408) 646-3915
FAX (408) 373-1634

March 29, 1991

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Opinion

Dear Sirs:

Please accept this as a request for written advice of the
Commission pursuant to Government Code §83114 (b) regarding the
following factual scenario.

The City of Monterey in its Charter establishes a
Neighborhood and Community Improvement Program which is funded
with a 16% of the City's transient occupancy tax. The total funded
budget of this program is currently about $1,400,000, and is to
be spent only on capital projects of community-wide benefits
within the various neighborhoods of the City.

The Charter establishes a Neighborhood Improvement Program
Committee (hereinafter "Committee") comprised of at least one
resident from each residential neighborhood in the City appointed
by the City Council. The Committee recommends a list of capital
improvements desired to be accomplished in each neighborhood to
the City Council. The Council does not have to fund each
project, but it may only expend this money on projects
recommended by this Committee.

The term "capital projects" as used in this program
includes, but is not limited to, streets, storm drains, sewers,
sidewalks, lighting, traffic control devices, landscaping and
beautification, parks, recreational facilities, and public
buildings. Obviously the inclusion of such improvements in a
neighborhood may have a material financial effect on the value of
residential property in recipient neighborhoods.

The City has determined that the Committee in its
deliberations is subject to the Ralph M. Brown Act as a
legislative body as that term is defined in Government Code

§54952.2. The City has also determined that the Committee
members must comply with our conflict of interest code and submit
annual FPPC Form 721 interest statements because of the direct
involvement in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have
a material effect on the financial interest of the person taking
action pursuant to Government Code §87302.

Based on this information, are Committee representatives
disqualified from participating in the selection of projects for
their own neighborhoods? All representatives live within the
vicinity of neighborhood projects (certainly within 2500 feet,
often within 300 feet), and many projects such as installation of
sidewalks, construction of parks or neighborhood centers, or
installation of street lighting has a direct and material effect
on property valuation although it is impossible to approximate
such values in the abstract in advance.

If representatives are disqualified from voting for projects
in their own neighborhood, are they similarly disqualified from
projects outside their representative area, but within 2500 feet
of their residence?

I have included a copy of a recent opinion of this office
regarding applicability of the Brown Act and conflict provisions
to this Committee as perhaps another source of information
regarding the operation of the Committee. I would be happy to
provide any further information needed to render your advice upon
request.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated courtesy and
cooperation.

Very truly yours,

William B. Conners
City Attorney

WBC:lk
cc: Mayor and Council
City Manager
City Clerk
Community Development Director
Engineering & Maintenance Director
Finance Director

CITY OF MONTEREY


To:     Mayor and City Councilmembers; City Manager

From:   City Attorney

Date:   March 15, 1991

Subject: Neighborhood and Community Improvement Committee

     It has been asked whether or not the Neighborhood and
Community Improvement Committee (herein NIP Committee) is an
official "legislative body" as that term is defined in the Ralph
M. Brown Act, and if so, is it subject to the terms and
conditions of that Act?

     A related question is whether or not the NIP Committee is
subject to the Political Reform Act of 1974 and thereby required
to submit conflict of interest forms.

     It is my opinion that the NIP Committee is a "legislative
body" and is therefore subject to the Brown Act, and members of
the NIP Committee should be required to complete and file
conflict of interest statements.

     The Ralph M. Brown Act, in Government Code §54952.2 defines
"legislative body" as including any multimember body which
exercises any authority delegated to it by the primary
legislative body. In this case, the NIP Committee is appointed
by the City Council pursuant to Charter §6.6 d. The Committee is
tasked with formulating a list of capital improvement projects
recommended to the Council for implementation. The City Council
then exercises its discretion in selecting, funding, and
constructing the improvements from the list.

     It is clear that the NIP Committee is a multimember body,
and that it acts in a legislative capacity in selecting
appropriate capital projects for possible implementation. While
the Charter limits the scope of their task, it is clear that
their function is one of delegated responsibility from the City
Council. The Committee falls squarely within the definition
established in §54952.2, and therefore all meetings of that body
shall conform to the remaining provisions of the Brown Act.

     The Political Reform Act of 1974 requires that local
agencies adopt conflict of interest codes to require local
officials who may be involved in the making of decisions which
may foreseeably have a material effect on a financial interest of
the person taking action (Government Code §87302). Monterey has
adopted such a code, and Key City employees and all Board and
Commission members are required to file a Fair Political
Practices Commission (FPPC) form each year setting forth

                                                    -3-/S-28

information related to potential conflicts of interest.  At
present, members of the NIP Committee have not been included as
required to participate.

     The stated intent of this section is that all employees,
officers, and consultants whose positions entail the making or
participation in the making of decisions which could foreseeably
have an impact on their own financial status must be included in
our conflict of interest code and file disclosure forms required
by the FPPC in a timely manner.  As noted above, members of the
NIP Committee are appointed by the Council in much the same
manner as are members of Boards and Commissions.  They
participate in the decision to allocate significant sums of money
to various neighborhood projects.  It may be possible, however
unlikely, that a member could attempt to influence the decision
of whether or not to include a project in the NIP program which
would have a financial impact on the individual.  For instance,
the member could vote to install sidewalks in various
neighborhoods while having a contract with the City to install
all sidewalks required during a fiscal year.  Other more
realistic examples could be presented as well, but what is
important is that the statute does constitute attempts to require by
anyone who might have a conflict situation as a result of their
government position, and I think it is clear that this could
arise.  For that reason, I believe our conflict of interest code
should be amended to include NIP Committee members, and they
should be required to fill out appropriate FPPC forms.

     It may be unfortunate that this body is required to comply
with these statutes, however, it does seem quite clear that the
laws cited do require participation.  The City Clerk will be
asked to amend the conflict of interest code, and the staff for
the Committee will be tasked with implementation of these two
acts.

                                        [signature]
                                        William B. Conners

WBC:lk

cc:  City Clerk
     Community Development Director
     Engineering & Maintenance Director
     Assistant City Manager
     Finance Director

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 4, 1991


William B. Conners
City Attorney
City of Monterey
City Hall
Monterey, CA  93940

Re:  Letter No. 91-206

Dear Mr. Conners:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on April 1, 1990 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission.  If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact Mark Moroni, an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice.  If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed.  If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can.  (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Scott Haliabrin
Acting General Counsel

sections.question: Based on this information, are Committee representatives disqualified from participating in the selection of projects for their own neighborhoods? All representatives live within the vicinity of neighborhood projects (certainly within 2500 feet, often within 300 feet), and many projects such as installation of sidewalks, construction of parks or neighborhood centers, or installation of street lighting has a direct and material effect on property valuation although it is impossible to approximate such values in the abstract in advance. If representatives are disqualified from voting for projects in their own neighborhood, are they similarly disqualified from projects outside their representative area, but within 2500 feet of their residence?
sections.question_synthetic: The City of Monterey seeks guidance on whether members of its Neighborhood Improvement Program Committee are disqualified from voting on capital projects in their own neighborhoods or within 2500 feet of their residences, given that such projects may materially affect property values and the members must file conflict of interest disclosures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This document contains both an incoming request from City Attorney William B. Conners (dated March 29, 1991) and a prior city attorney opinion memo (dated March 15, 1991), followed by an FPPC acknowledgment letter (April 4, 1991) indicating receipt but not providing the actual advice. The advice letter itself is not included in this document. The FPPC acknowledgment references Letter No. 91-206.

If representatives are disqualified from voting for projects in their own neighborhood, are they similarly disqualified from projects outside their representative area, but within 2500 feet of their residence?

embedding.summary: The City of Monterey, represented by its City Attorney, submitted a request for written advice from the California FPPC regarding conflict of interest disqualifications for members of the Neighborhood Improvement Program Committee, specifically whether they can vote on projects affecting their own neighborhoods. The FPPC acknowledged receipt of the request on April 4, 1991, and stated it would provide a response within 21 working days.
